1:
"Know ye not, brethren, (for I speak to them that know the law,) how that the law hath dominion over a man as long as he liveth?" The witnesses note the principle: the law binds only the living. On "I speak to them that know the law," the grammatical voices note the audience. Barnes and Robertson note Paul addresses those acquainted with law (Jews and others familiar with legal principles), appealing to a known maxim.

On "the law hath dominion over a man as long as he liveth," the witnesses note the principle. Law binds a person only while he lives; death dissolves the law's claim — a universal legal principle Paul will apply to the believer's relation to the law.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the purpose: to show, by the marriage analogy (vv.2-3), that the believer, having died with Christ, is freed from the law's dominion. The verse displays the principle: the law binds only the living.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul addresses those who know the law (Jews and others familiar with legal principles, Barnes, Robertson), appealing to the known maxim that the law has dominion over a person only as long as he lives — death dissolving the law's claim. Henry and Calvin note the purpose: to show by the marriage analogy (vv.2-3) that the believer, having died with Christ, is freed from the law's dominion. The verse displays the principle. The verse marks the audience: those who know the law. The verse displays the maxim: the law binds the living. The verse displays the dissolution: death ends the claim. The verse displays the purpose: the believer's freedom. The principle displays the foundation for the believer's release from the law. The verse displays the legal axiom: law binds the living. The verse displays the limit: as long as he lives. The verse displays the application to come: freedom by death. The principle that the law has dominion over a man only as long as he lives displays the legal foundation for the believer's release from the law: as death dissolves the law's claim, so the Christian, having died with Christ, is freed from the law's dominion. The verse displays the universal maxim Paul appeals to, and applies it through the marriage analogy (vv.2-3).
2:
"For the woman which hath an husband is bound by the law to her husband so long as he liveth; but if the husband be dead, she is loosed from the law of her husband." The witnesses note the marriage analogy. On "bound by the law to her husband," the grammatical voices note the illustration. Barnes and Robertson note the married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives — an example of the principle (v.1) that law binds during life.

On "if the husband be dead, she is loosed," the witnesses note the release. His death frees her from the marriage law — she is no longer bound, free to marry another.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the application to come: the believer is like the woman, freed by a death (her own, in union with Christ) to be joined to another, Christ. The verse displays the marriage analogy: death frees the wife from the marriage law.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives, but his death frees her from the marriage law (Barnes, Robertson) — an illustration of the principle (v.1) that law binds during life. Calvin and Theodoret note the application: the believer, like the woman, is freed by a death to be joined to another, Christ. The verse displays the marriage analogy. The verse marks the bond: the wife bound while he lives. The verse displays the release: freed by his death. The verse displays the principle: law binds during life. The verse displays the application: freedom to remarry. The marriage analogy displays the dissolution of legal bond by death. The verse displays the binding: by the marriage law. The verse displays the loosing: by death. The verse displays the freedom: to marry another. The marriage analogy — the wife bound to her husband while he lives, loosed by his death — displays the dissolution of a legal bond by death, illustrating the principle that law binds only during life. The verse sets up the application that the believer, freed by death from the law, may be joined to another (Christ), and works out the analogy (v.3).
3:
"So then if, while her husband liveth, she be married to another man, she shall be called an adulteress: but if her husband be dead, she is free from that law; so that she is no adulteress, though she be married to another man." The brief verse completes the marriage analogy. The grammatical voices note the two cases. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast: married to another while the husband lives, she is an adulteress; but if he is dead, she is free and no adulteress in marrying another.

On the point, the witnesses note the legal clarity. The death of the husband alone dissolves the bond and removes the charge of adultery — death is the decisive release.

The eastern Theodoret notes the application: only death frees from the marriage law, as only death (with Christ) frees the believer from the law to be joined to Christ. The verse displays the completed analogy: death alone frees to a new union.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: married to another while the husband lives, she is an adulteress; but if he is dead, she is free and no adulteress in marrying another (Barnes, Robertson) — the death of the husband alone dissolving the bond and removing the charge. Theodoret notes the application: only death frees from the marriage law, as only death (with Christ) frees the believer to be joined to Christ. The verse displays the completed analogy. The verse marks the two cases: adulteress, or free. The verse displays the decisive factor: the husband's death. The verse displays the release: free to remarry. The verse displays the application: death frees to Christ. The completed analogy displays death as the decisive release to a new union. The verse displays the bond: broken only by death. The verse displays the freedom: to a new marriage. The verse displays the parallel: with the believer and Christ. The completed marriage analogy — death alone frees the wife to a new union without the charge of adultery — displays death as the decisive release that dissolves the legal bond. The verse sets up the application: the believer, having died with Christ, is freed from the law to be joined to another, the risen Christ (v.4), that this new union might bear fruit unto God.
4:
"Wherefore, my brethren, ye also are become dead to the law by the body of Christ; that ye should be married to another, even to him who is raised from the dead, that we should bring forth fruit unto God." The witnesses note the application: dead to the law, married to Christ. On "dead to the law by the body of Christ," the grammatical voices note the means. Barnes and Robertson note the believer has become dead to the law through the body (the crucified death) of Christ — by union with his death, the believer died to the law's claim.

On "married to another, even to him who is raised," the witnesses note the new union. Freed by death from the law, the believer is joined to the risen Christ — a new marriage, as the widow to a new husband.

On "bring forth fruit unto God," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the purpose: this union with Christ is fruitful, producing holiness and good works unto God — the fruit of the new marriage. The verse displays the application: dead to the law, married to the risen Christ, to bear fruit unto God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer has become dead to the law through the body (the crucified death) of Christ (by union with his death, Barnes, Robertson), that he might be married to another — the risen Christ, a new union as the widow to a new husband — to bring forth fruit unto God. Calvin and Theodoret note the purpose: this union with Christ is fruitful, producing holiness and good works. The verse displays the application. The verse marks the death: to the law by Christ's body. The verse displays the union: married to the risen Christ. The verse displays the purpose: fruit unto God. The verse displays the fruitfulness: holiness and good works. The application displays the believer's release from the law and union with Christ. The verse displays the freedom: dead to the law. The verse displays the marriage: to the risen Christ. The verse displays the fruit: unto God. The application — dead to the law by the body of Christ, married to the risen Christ to bring forth fruit unto God — displays the believer's release from the law and new union with Christ: as the widow freed by death may marry another, so the believer, having died with Christ to the law, is joined to the risen Lord in a fruitful union that produces holiness unto God. The verse displays the fruitful marriage of the soul to Christ, and explains the contrast between the old and new states (vv.5-6).
5:
"For when we were in the flesh, the motions of sins, which were by the law, did work in our members to bring forth fruit unto death." The witnesses note the former state in the flesh. On "when we were in the flesh," the grammatical voices note the unregenerate state. Barnes and Robertson note "in the flesh" means the unrenewed, carnal state before conversion — under sin's power.

On "the motions of sins, which were by the law," the witnesses note the law's effect. The sinful passions, stirred up (occasioned) by the law (which by forbidding excites the rebellious heart), worked in the members.

On "fruit unto death," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the issue: this old state bore fruit unto death — the opposite of the fruit unto God (v.4). The verse displays the former state: sinful passions, stirred by the law, bearing fruit unto death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: when in the flesh (the unrenewed, carnal state before conversion, Barnes, Robertson), the sinful passions (the motions of sins), stirred up or occasioned by the law (which by forbidding excites the rebellious heart), worked in the members to bring forth fruit unto death — the opposite of the fruit unto God (v.4, Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the former state. The verse marks the state: in the flesh. The verse displays the passions: stirred by the law. The verse displays the working: in the members. The verse displays the fruit: unto death. The former state displays the deadly working of sin under the law. The verse displays the carnality: in the flesh. The verse displays the excitement: sin stirred by the law. The verse displays the harvest: death. The former state — sinful passions, stirred up by the law, working in the members to bring forth fruit unto death — displays the deadly working of sin in the unregenerate: the law, by forbidding, excited the rebellious passions, which bore fruit unto death. The verse displays the contrast with the new state's fruit unto God, and the law's role in provoking sin in the carnal heart, leading to the believer's deliverance from the law (v.6).
6:
"But now we are delivered from the law, that being dead wherein we were held; that we should serve in newness of spirit, and not in the oldness of the letter." The witnesses note deliverance from the law unto new service. On "delivered from the law," the grammatical voices note the release. Barnes and Robertson note the believer is now delivered (released, discharged) from the law — having died to that which held him in bondage.

On "that being dead wherein we were held," the witnesses note the manner. Having died (to the law, with Christimony), the believer is released from the law's binding power — the bond dissolved by death.

On "serve in newness of spirit, not oldness of letter," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the new service: the believer now serves God in the newness of the Spirit (inward, willing, living obedience) rather than the oldness of the letter (the mere external, dead bondage of the law). The verse displays deliverance from the law unto the new service of the Spirit.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the believer is now delivered (released, discharged) from the law, having died to that which held him in bondage (the bond dissolved by death, Barnes, Robertson), that he should serve in newness of spirit (inward, willing, living obedience by the Spirit) and not in the oldness of the letter (the external, dead bondage of the law, Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays deliverance from the law unto new service. The verse marks the release: delivered from the law. The verse displays the manner: dead to what held us. The verse displays the new service: newness of spirit. The verse displays the contrast: spirit vs letter. The deliverance displays the believer's freedom for living, spiritual service. The verse displays the release: from the law's bondage. The verse displays the death: to what held us. The verse displays the service: in the Spirit. Deliverance from the law unto the new service of the Spirit displays the believer's freedom: released by death from the law that held him in bondage, he now serves God in the newness of the Spirit — inward, willing, living obedience — rather than the oldness of the letter, the dead external bondage. The verse displays the gospel's transformation of service from law to Spirit, and raises the question whether the law is therefore sin (v.7).
7:
"What shall we say then? Is the law sin? God forbid. Nay, I had not known sin, but by the law: for I had not known lust, except the law had said, Thou shalt not covet." The witnesses note the vindication of the law: it reveals sin. On "is the law sin?" the grammatical voices note the objection. Barnes and Henry note the natural cavil from vv.5-6: if the law stirs sin, is the law itself sinful? Paul rejects it ("God forbid").

On "I had not known sin but by the law," the witnesses note the law's revealing office. The law makes sin known — defines it, exposes it; Paul knew lust (coveting) to be sin only because the law said, "Thou shalt not covet."

On the example, the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the inward reach: the tenth commandment (against coveting) reaches the heart, revealing inward sin; the law's office is to disclose sin, not to cause it. The verse displays the vindication of the law: it reveals, not produces, sin.

The witnesses agree: to the cavil "is the law sin?" (the natural objection from vv.5-6, Barnes, Henry), Paul replies "God forbid" — for the law's office is to reveal sin: "I had not known sin but by the law," knowing lust (coveting) to be sin only because the law said "Thou shalt not covet." Calvin and Wesley note the tenth commandment reaches the heart, revealing inward sin; the law discloses sin, not causes it. The verse displays the vindication of the law. The verse marks the objection: is the law sin? The verse displays the rejection: God forbid. The verse displays the office: the law reveals sin. The verse displays the example: thou shalt not covet. The vindication displays the law's revealing, not corrupting, function. The verse displays the cavil: the law sinful? The verse displays the answer: it makes sin known. The verse displays the inward reach: against coveting. The vindication of the law — that it reveals rather than produces sin — displays the law's holy office: far from being sin, the law makes sin known, defining and exposing it, so that Paul knew inward lust to be sin only because the commandment forbade coveting. The verse displays the law's convicting function reaching even the heart, and explains how sin used the commandment (vv.8-11).
8:
"But sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought in me all manner of concupiscence. For without the law sin was dead." The witnesses note sin's use of the commandment. On "taking occasion by the commandment," the grammatical voices note the abuse. Barnes and Robertson note sin (the indwelling principle) seized the commandment as an occasion (a starting-point, a base of operations) to provoke all manner of evil desire — the forbidding of a thing exciting the corrupt heart to crave it.

On "wrought all manner of concupiscence," the witnesses note the effect. Sin, occasioned by the prohibition, stirred up every kind of evil desire (concupiscence, coveting) — the rebellion of the heart against the command.

On "without the law sin was dead," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the dormancy: apart from the law, sin lay comparatively dormant (dead, inactive, unfelt) — not non-existent, but not roused or recognized. The verse displays sin using the commandment to provoke evil desire.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: sin (the indwelling principle) took occasion by the commandment (a starting-point, a base of operations) to provoke all manner of concupiscence (evil desire) — the forbidding of a thing exciting the corrupt heart to crave it (Barnes, Robertson) — and without the law sin was dead (comparatively dormant, inactive, unfelt, not non-existent but unroused, Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays sin's use of the commandment. The verse marks the abuse: sin taking occasion. The verse displays the effect: all concupiscence wrought. The verse displays the dormancy: sin dead without the law. The verse displays the provocation: forbidding exciting desire. Sin's use of the commandment displays the perversity of the corrupt heart. The verse displays the occasion: the commandment seized. The verse displays the stirring: evil desire provoked. The verse displays the dormancy: sin unroused without law. Sin taking occasion by the commandment to work all concupiscence, while without the law sin was dead, displays the perversity of the corrupt heart: the very prohibition becomes sin's base of operations, exciting the forbidden desire, whereas apart from the law sin lay comparatively dormant and unrecognized. The verse displays how the holy law, through the corruption of the heart, occasions the revival of sin, and describes its deadly effect (vv.9-11).
9:
"For I was alive without the law once: but when the commandment came, sin revived, and I died." The witnesses note the coming of the commandment and the death of the self. On "I was alive without the law once," the grammatical voices debate the reference. Barnes and Robertson note this may mean Paul's earlier life (childhood, or before the law came home to his conscience) when he felt secure, "alive" in a false sense — self-complacent, unawakened.

On "when the commandment came, sin revived, and I died," the witnesses note the awakening. When the commandment came home to the conscience (in its spiritual force), sin revived (sprang to life, was felt and roused) and the self died — the false security shattered, the sense of condemnation come.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the experience: the law, coming in power, slays self-righteous complacency, revealing sin and bringing the sentence of death to the conscience. The verse displays the coming of the commandment, the revival of sin, the death of the self.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "I was alive without the law once" refers to a state of false security (Paul's earlier life, or before the law came home to the conscience) — self-complacent, unawakened (Barnes, Robertson) — but when the commandment came home in its spiritual force, sin revived (sprang to life, was felt) and the self died (the false security shattered, condemnation come). Calvin and Theodoret note the law slays self-righteous complacency, revealing sin and bringing the sentence of death to the conscience. The verse displays the coming of the commandment. The verse marks the false life: alive without the law. The verse displays the awakening: the commandment came. The verse displays the revival: sin sprang to life. The verse displays the death: of the complacent self. The coming of the commandment displays the law's power to awaken and slay. The verse displays the false security: alive without law. The verse displays the rousing: sin revived. The verse displays the death: self-complacency slain. The coming of the commandment, the revival of sin, and the death of the self display the law's power to awaken and condemn: while seemingly "alive" in false security, the coming of the commandment in its spiritual force roused sin to life and slew the complacent self, bringing the sense of condemnation. The verse displays the experience of conviction under the law, and how sin used the commandment to deceive and kill (vv.10-11).
10:
"And the commandment, which was ordained to life, I found to be unto death." The brief verse notes the commandment's unintended effect. The grammatical voices note the paradox. Barnes and Robertson note the commandment was ordained (designed) to give life (had life been kept, "do this and live"), yet through sin it proved to be unto death — not by its own fault but through human corruption.

On the meaning, the witnesses note the reversal. The law that promised life to the obedient became, to the sinner, an instrument of death — revealing sin and pronouncing condemnation.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the cause: not the law's fault but sin's; the good law, meeting a corrupt heart, issues in death. The verse displays the commandment, ordained to life, found to be unto death.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the commandment was ordained (designed) to give life ("do this and live"), yet through sin it proved to be unto death (Barnes, Robertson) — not by its own fault but through human corruption; the law that promised life to the obedient became, to the sinner, an instrument of death. Calvin and Theodoret note the cause is not the law's fault but sin's: the good law, meeting a corrupt heart, issues in death. The verse displays the commandment's unintended effect. The verse marks the design: ordained to life. The verse displays the reversal: found unto death. The verse displays the cause: human corruption. The verse displays the vindication: not the law's fault. The unintended effect displays the law's death-dealing power against the sinner. The verse displays the purpose: life intended. The verse displays the result: death found. The verse displays the cause: sin, not the law. The commandment, ordained to life yet found to be unto death, displays the paradoxical effect of the holy law upon the sinner: designed to give life to the obedient, it became, through human corruption, an instrument of death, revealing sin and pronouncing condemnation. The verse displays that the fault lies not in the law but in sin, and explains how sin wrought this death (v.11).
11:
"For sin, taking occasion by the commandment, deceived me, and by it slew me." The witnesses note sin's deceit and slaying through the commandment. On "taking occasion by the commandment," the grammatical voices note the repeated abuse. Barnes and Robertson note sin again seized the commandment as its occasion (as in v.8) — this time to deceive and kill.

On "deceived me," the witnesses note the deception. Sin deceived (beguiled, as the serpent in Eden) — promising pleasure or freedom, but leading to ruin; the deceit of sin using the very commandment.

On "by it slew me," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the death: sin, by means of the commandment, slew the sinner — bringing condemnation and the sense of death. The allusion to Eden (Genesis 3) is noted by several. The verse displays sin deceiving and slaying through the commandment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: sin again took occasion by the commandment (as in v.8) to deceive (beguile, as the serpent in Eden, promising pleasure but leading to ruin) and by it slay the sinner — bringing condemnation and death (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin and Theodoret note sin, by means of the commandment, slew the sinner; the allusion to Eden (Genesis 3) is marked. The verse displays sin's deceit and slaying. The verse marks the occasion: by the commandment. The verse displays the deceit: sin beguiling. The verse displays the slaying: death brought. The verse displays the allusion: to Eden. Sin's deceit and slaying display the deadly cunning of indwelling sin. The verse displays the abuse: the commandment seized. The verse displays the beguiling: sin deceiving. The verse displays the death: the sinner slain. Sin deceiving and slaying through the commandment displays the deadly cunning of indwelling sin: as the serpent beguiled Eve, so sin, taking the very commandment as its occasion, deceives the sinner and by it brings death and condemnation. The verse displays the Edenic pattern of sin's deceit working through the good commandment, and clears the law of blame by laying it all on sin, leading to the law's vindication (v.12).
12:
"Wherefore the law is holy, and the commandment holy, and just, and good." The witnesses note the vindication of the law: holy, just, and good. On "the law is holy," the grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having shown that the fault lies in sin not the law, concludes that the law (and the particular commandment) is holy — pure, from a holy God.

On "holy, and just, and good," the witnesses note the three-fold praise. The commandment is holy (in its nature), just (in its requirements), and good (in its design and tendency) — wholly excellent.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the full acquittal: the law is completely vindicated; whatever death it occasioned is sin's doing, not the law's, which remains holy, just, and good. The verse displays the vindication: the law holy, just, and good.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes (the fault being in sin, not the law) that the law and the commandment are holy (pure, from a holy God), just (in their requirements), and good (in their design and tendency) — wholly excellent (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin and Theodoret note the full acquittal: whatever death the law occasioned is sin's doing, the law remaining holy, just, and good. The verse displays the vindication. The verse marks the conclusion: the law is holy. The verse displays the three-fold praise: holy, just, good. The verse displays the acquittal: the law cleared. The verse displays the excellence: wholly good. The vindication displays the law's complete excellence. The verse displays the holiness: from a holy God. The verse displays the justice: in its requirements. The verse displays the goodness: in its design. The vindication of the law — holy, and just, and good — displays its complete excellence: having shown that the fault for death lies wholly in sin and not in the law, Paul fully acquits the commandment, praising it as holy in nature, just in requirement, and good in design. The verse displays the threefold commendation of the law over against the corruption of sin, and presses the question how the good law brought death (v.13).
13:
"Was then that which is good made death unto me? God forbid. But sin, that it might appear sin, working death in me by that which is good; that sin by the commandment might become exceeding sinful." The witnesses note the purpose: sin shown exceeding sinful. On "was that which is good made death?" the grammatical voices note the objection. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks whether the good law became the cause of death — and rejects it ("God forbid"); it was sin, not the law, that wrought death.

On "that it might appear sin... working death by the good," the witnesses note the unmasking. Sin is unmasked, shown in its true colors, by working death through the good law — the contrast revealing sin's malignity.

On "exceeding sinful," the Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the purpose: that sin, by perverting the good commandment unto death, might appear exceeding sinful (sinful beyond measure) — the law's effect being to expose the enormity of sin. The verse displays the purpose: sin unmasked as exceeding sinful by the good law.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: to the objection whether the good law became death, Paul replies "God forbid" — it was sin, not the law, that wrought death (Barnes, Robertson); sin is unmasked, shown in its true colors, by working death through the good law, that sin by the commandment might appear exceeding sinful (sinful beyond measure, Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the purpose: sin shown exceeding sinful. The verse marks the objection: the good made death? The verse displays the rejection: God forbid. The verse displays the unmasking: sin shown by the good. The verse displays the purpose: exceeding sinful. The purpose displays the law's office to expose the enormity of sin. The verse displays the culprit: sin, not the law. The verse displays the contrast: sin against the good. The verse displays the exposure: sin's malignity. The purpose — that sin, working death through the good law, might appear exceeding sinful — displays the law's office to unmask the enormity of sin: not the good commandment but sin wrought death, and the very contrast reveals sin in its true, exceeding sinfulness. The verse displays how the holy law, by occasion of sin's perversion, exposes the depth of human corruption, and leads to the inward conflict of the law and the flesh (vv.14-25).
14:
"For we know that the law is spiritual: but I am carnal, sold under sin." The witnesses note the spiritual law and the carnal self — and the opening of the great debate. On "the law is spiritual," the grammatical voices note the praise. Chrysostom and Barnes note the law is spiritual — from the Spirit, reaching the spirit, a teacher of virtue; the fault for sin lies not in the holy law but in the carnal disciple.

On "I am carnal, sold under sin," the witnesses note the confession — and the crux. The "I" is carnal (fleshly), sold under sin (as a slave). Here begins the great debate: who is the "I"? Henry frames the controversy: some take it of the convinced-but-unregenerate soul, others of the renewed-but-imperfect believer.

The debate divides the schools. The Reformed Calvin and Gill (with Augustine's later view) hold it describes the regenerate believer — the normal Christian struggle with indwelling sin; the eastern Chrysostom and the wesleyan-arminian tradition tend to read the unregenerate man under the law or Paul before conversion. The verse displays the spiritual law, the carnal self, and the opening of the great conflict.

The witnesses agree the law is spiritual (from the Spirit, reaching the spirit, a teacher of virtue, Chrysostom, Barnes) while the "I" is carnal, sold under sin — and here the great debate over Romans 7 opens. Henry frames the controversy: the conflict described is taken by some of the convinced-but-unregenerate soul, by others of the renewed-but-imperfect believer. The Reformed Calvin and Gill (with Augustine's mature view) hold it portrays the regenerate believer's normal struggle with indwelling sin; the eastern Chrysostom and the Arminian tradition lean to the unregenerate under the law or Paul before conversion. The verse displays the spiritual law and the carnal self. The verse marks the praise: the law spiritual. The verse displays the confession: I am carnal. The verse displays the crux: who is the "I"? The verse displays the bondage: sold under sin. The spiritual law and carnal self display the conflict that opens the great debate. The verse displays the holiness: the law spiritual. The verse displays the weakness: the self carnal. The verse displays the controversy: regenerate or not. The spiritual law and the carnal self, sold under sin, display the conflict at the heart of Romans 7 and open its great interpretive debate: the law is wholly spiritual, but the "I" is carnal and enslaved — and whether this "I" is the convinced-but-unregenerate man or the renewed-but-imperfect believer is the central controversy that governs the reading of the whole passage (vv.14-25). The verse displays the radical contrast between the holy law and the flesh.
15:
"For that which I do I allow not: for what I would, that do I not; but what I hate, that do I." The witnesses note the inward division of the will. On "that which I do I allow not," the grammatical voices note the contradiction. Barnes and Robertson note the divided self: the "I" does what it does not approve (allow) — acting against its own better judgment.

On "what I would I do not, what I hate I do," the witnesses note the conflict. There is a war between the will and the deed: willing good but not doing it, hating evil yet doing it — the painful experience of inward division.

The schools read it by their view of the "I." The Reformed Calvin (regenerate view) sees the believer grieving over indwelling sin's resistance to the renewed will; the wesleyan-arminian and eastern readers see the convinced sinner's helpless struggle under conviction. The verse displays the inward division: doing what one hates, not doing what one wills.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree the verse describes the inward division of the will: the "I" does what it does not approve, wills good but does it not, hates evil yet does it — a war between will and deed (Barnes, Robertson). The schools read it by their view of the "I": Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer grieving over indwelling sin's resistance to the renewed will; the Arminian and eastern readers see the convinced sinner's helpless struggle under conviction. The verse displays the inward division. The verse marks the contradiction: doing what one allows not. The verse displays the conflict: will vs deed. The verse displays the war: good willed, evil done. The verse displays the reading: by the view of the "I." The inward division displays the painful struggle within the self. The verse displays the disapproval: acting against judgment. The verse displays the failure: good not done. The verse displays the bondage: evil done. The inward division of the will — doing what one hates, not doing what one wills — displays the painful struggle within the self, whether of the regenerate believer wrestling with indwelling sin (the Reformed view) or the convinced sinner helpless under conviction (the Arminian/eastern view). The verse displays the experiential core of the Romans 7 conflict, the divided self at war with itself, and continues the analysis of the inward war (vv.16-20).
16:
"If then I do that which I would not, I consent unto the law that it is good." The brief verse notes the will's consent to the law. The grammatical voices note the inference. Barnes and Robertson note that in doing what the "I" would not do (disapproving its own act), the self thereby consents to (agrees with) the law that it is good — the very reluctance proving agreement with the law's standard.

On the argument, the witnesses note the vindication. The inward disapproval of sin shows that the will sides with the law, confirming the law's goodness (v.12) — the conscience and the law agreeing against the deed.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the point: the struggle itself testifies to the law's goodness and the better self's assent to it. The verse displays the will's consent to the goodness of the law.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: in doing what the "I" would not (disapproving its own act), the self consents to the law that it is good (Barnes, Robertson) — the very reluctance proving agreement with the law's standard, the conscience and the law siding against the deed. Calvin and Theodoret note the struggle itself testifies to the law's goodness and the better self's assent. The verse displays the will's consent to the law. The verse marks the inference: consenting to the law. The verse displays the proof: reluctance shows agreement. The verse displays the vindication: the law good. The verse displays the assent: the will siding with the law. The will's consent displays the inward agreement with the law's goodness. The verse displays the disapproval: of the deed. The verse displays the agreement: with the law. The verse displays the testimony: to the law's goodness. The will's consent to the goodness of the law — for in doing what it would not, the self agrees that the law is good — displays the inward testimony of the conscience: the very reluctance and disapproval prove that the better self sides with the law against the sinful deed, confirming the law's goodness. The verse displays how the inward conflict itself vindicates the holy law, and locates the cause of sin in indwelling sin (v.17).
17:
"Now then it is no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me." The witnesses note the locating of sin in indwelling sin. On "no more I that do it," the grammatical voices note the distinction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul distinguishes the true self (the renewed will, or the better judgment) from the sin that does the evil — "not I, but sin."

On "sin that dwelleth in me," the witnesses note indwelling sin. The cause is the sin that dwells (resides, has lodged) in the person — the remaining principle of corruption; not excusing the self but locating the evil in the indwelling enemy.

The schools read it by their view: the Reformed Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer disowning the sin that wars against his renewed self; the wesleyan-arminian and eastern note the danger of excusing self, but read the convicted sinner recognizing sin's power. The verse displays the locating of the evil in indwelling sin.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul distinguishes the true self from the sin that does the evil — "not I, but sin that dwelleth in me" (the remaining principle of corruption, residing in the person, Barnes, Robertson). The schools read it variously: Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer disowning the sin that wars against his renewed self; the Arminian and eastern note the convicted sinner recognizing sin's power (with care not to excuse self). The verse displays the locating of sin. The verse marks the distinction: not I, but sin. The verse displays the cause: indwelling sin. The verse displays the corruption: residing within. The verse displays the reading: by the view of the "I." The locating of sin displays the distinction between the self and indwelling sin. The verse displays the disowning: not the true self. The verse displays the enemy: sin within. The verse displays the lodging: dwelling in me. The locating of the evil in indwelling sin — "no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me" — displays the distinction between the true self and the indwelling principle of corruption: the evil is traced not to the renewed will but to the sin that resides within, the believer disowning it (Reformed) or the convicted sinner recognizing its power. The verse displays the deep diagnosis of indwelling sin as the source of the inward conflict, and continues with the helplessness of the flesh (v.18).
18:
"For I know that in me (that is, in my flesh,) dwelleth no good thing: for to will is present with me; but how to perform that which is good I find not." The witnesses note the helplessness of the flesh. On "in my flesh dwelleth no good thing," the grammatical voices note the confession. Barnes and Robertson note Paul confesses that in his flesh (the unrenewed nature) there dwells no good thing — total depravity of the flesh, no good to be found there.

On "to will is present, but how to perform I find not," the witnesses note the gap. The will to good is present (there is a desire for good), but the power to perform it is lacking — the will outruns the ability.

The schools read it by their view: the Reformed Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer with a renewed will yet unable, through remaining flesh, to perform the good perfectly; the puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian read the helplessness of the convicted soul. The verse displays the helplessness of the flesh: the will present, the power lacking.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul confesses that in his flesh (the unrenewed nature) dwells no good thing — total depravity of the flesh (Barnes, Robertson) — and that the will to good is present but the power to perform it is lacking, the will outrunning the ability. The schools read it variously: Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer with a renewed will yet unable, through remaining flesh, to perform the good perfectly; Henry and the Arminian read the helplessness of the convicted soul. The verse displays the helplessness of the flesh. The verse marks the confession: no good in the flesh. The verse displays the gap: will present, power lacking. The verse displays the desire: to will is present. The verse displays the inability: how to perform I find not. The helplessness displays the impotence of the flesh to do good. The verse displays the depravity: no good thing. The verse displays the willing: present. The verse displays the failure: cannot perform. The helplessness of the flesh — no good thing dwelling there, the will to good present but the power to perform lacking — displays the impotence of the unrenewed nature: even where good is willed, the flesh cannot perform it, the desire outrunning the ability. The verse displays the deep confession of human helplessness apart from grace, whether of the believer's remaining flesh or the convicted sinner's impotence, and restates the conflict (vv.19-20).
19:
"For the good that I would I do not: but the evil which I would not, that I do." The brief verse restates the inward conflict. The grammatical voices note the repetition for emphasis. Barnes and Robertson note Paul restates the painful contradiction (v.15): the good willed is not done, the evil not willed is done — the gap between desire and performance.

On the conflict, the witnesses note the helpless struggle. The self is caught in a war it cannot win by its own strength: willing good, doing evil.

The schools read it by their view of the "I." The Reformed Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer's grief at falling short; the wesleyan-arminian and eastern read the bondage of the convicted, unaided soul. The verse displays the restated conflict: good willed not done, evil not willed done.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul restates the painful contradiction (v.15) for emphasis — the good willed is not done, the evil not willed is done (Barnes, Robertson), the gap between desire and performance, a helpless struggle. The schools read it by their view: Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer's grief at falling short; the Arminian and eastern read the bondage of the convicted, unaided soul. The verse displays the restated conflict. The verse marks the repetition: for emphasis. The verse displays the gap: good willed, not done. The verse displays the reversal: evil not willed, done. The verse displays the reading: by the view of the "I." The restated conflict displays the depth of the inward struggle. The verse displays the desire: good willed. The verse displays the failure: not done. The verse displays the bondage: evil done. The restated inward conflict — the good willed not done, the evil not willed done — displays the depth and persistence of the struggle, whether the believer's grief at falling short of his renewed desire (Reformed) or the bondage of the convicted soul unaided by grace (Arminian/eastern). The verse displays the unrelenting war between will and deed at the heart of Romans 7, and again locates the cause in indwelling sin (v.20).
20:
"Now if I do that I would not, it is no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me." The brief verse restates the locating of sin in indwelling sin. The grammatical voices note the repetition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul again concludes (as v.17) that the evil is done not by the true self but by indwelling sin — emphasizing the distinction.

On the argument, the witnesses note the diagnosis. The doing of the unwilled evil proves that an alien power, indwelling sin, is at work — distinct from the willing self.

The schools read it by their view: the Reformed Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer disowning the indwelling sin; the wesleyan-arminian reads the convicted sinner recognizing sin's mastery. The verse displays the restated locating of sin in indwelling sin.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul again concludes (as v.17) that the unwilled evil is done not by the true self but by indwelling sin (Barnes, Robertson) — the doing of the unwilled evil proving an alien power at work, distinct from the willing self. The schools read it by their view: Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer disowning the indwelling sin; the Arminian reads the convicted sinner recognizing sin's mastery. The verse displays the restated locating of sin. The verse marks the repetition: not I, but sin. The verse displays the diagnosis: an alien power. The verse displays the distinction: self vs sin. The verse displays the reading: by the view of the "I." The restated locating displays the persistent distinction between the self and indwelling sin. The verse displays the disowning: not the true self. The verse displays the enemy: sin within. The verse displays the mastery: sin's power. The restated locating of sin in indwelling sin — "no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me" — displays the persistent distinction between the willing self and the indwelling principle of corruption: the unwilled evil proves an alien power at work, which the believer disowns (Reformed) or the convicted sinner recognizes as his master (Arminian). The verse displays the emphatic diagnosis of indwelling sin, and draws out the law of inward conflict (vv.21-23).
21:
"I find then a law, that, when I would do good, evil is present with me." The witnesses note the law of inward conflict. On "I find then a law," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul finds a "law" — not the Mosaic law here, but a fixed principle or rule operating within: that when he would do good, evil is present.

On "evil is present with me," the witnesses note the constant adversary. Whenever the will turns to good, evil (indwelling sin) is at hand, ready to oppose — a settled, recurring experience.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret note the diagnosis: this is the abiding law of the conflict between the renewed will and the remaining corruption (regenerate view) or the helpless bondage of the convicted (other view). The verse displays the law of inward conflict: evil present when good is willed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul finds a "law" — a fixed principle or rule operating within (not the Mosaic law here, Barnes, Robertson) — that when he would do good, evil (indwelling sin) is present, ever at hand to oppose, a settled recurring experience. Calvin and Theodoret note this is the abiding law of the conflict between the renewed will and remaining corruption (regenerate view) or the bondage of the convicted (other view). The verse displays the law of inward conflict. The verse marks the principle: a law within. The verse displays the adversary: evil present. The verse displays the timing: when good is willed. The verse displays the constancy: a settled experience. The law of inward conflict displays the abiding presence of opposing evil. The verse displays the principle: a fixed rule. The verse displays the opposition: evil at hand. The verse displays the diagnosis: the conflict's law. The law of inward conflict — that when the self would do good, evil is present — displays the abiding principle of the struggle: indwelling sin is ever at hand to oppose the will to good, a settled and recurring experience whether of the believer's remaining corruption (Reformed) or the convicted sinner's bondage. The verse displays the fixed rule of the inward war, and unfolds the two laws contending in the man (vv.22-23).
22:
"For I delight in the law of God after the inward man:" The witnesses note the delight in God's law in the inward man. On "I delight in the law of God," the grammatical voices note the affection. Barnes and Robertson note the "I" delights in (takes pleasure in, approves and loves) the law of God — a positive, hearty assent and love, not mere reluctant agreement.

On "after the inward man," the witnesses note the seat. This delight is in the inward man (the renewed mind, the higher self) — the inner being siding with God's law.

The debate sharpens here. The Reformed Calvin and Gill note that to "delight in the law of God after the inward man" is the mark of the regenerate — only the renewed heart truly delights in God's law (Psalm 1); this strongly favors the believer view. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke counters that even an unregenerate, conscientious Jew may approve and delight in the law (Isaiah 58:2). The verse displays the delight in God's law in the inward man.

The witnesses agree the "I" delights in (loves, heartily approves) the law of God after the inward man (the renewed mind, the higher self, Barnes, Robertson) — and here the great debate sharpens. The Reformed Calvin and Gill note that genuine delight in God's law after the inward man is the mark of the regenerate (only the renewed heart truly delights in the law, Psalm 1), strongly favoring the believer view; the wesleyan-arminian Clarke counters that even a conscientious unregenerate Jew may approve and delight in the law (Isaiah 58:2). The verse displays the delight in God's law. The verse marks the affection: I delight. The verse displays the seat: the inward man. The verse displays the debate: regenerate mark or not. The verse displays the assent: the inner being for God's law. The delight displays the inward man's love for God's law. The verse displays the pleasure: in the law. The verse displays the seat: the renewed mind. The verse displays the controversy: the mark of grace. The delight in God's law after the inward man displays the positive love of the inner being for the holy law — a verse that sharpens the great debate: the Reformed see in genuine delight in God's law the unmistakable mark of the regenerate heart, while the Arminian holds even a conscientious unregenerate man may approve the law. The verse displays the higher self's hearty assent to God's law, set against the war in the members (v.23).
23:
"But I see another law in my members, warring against the law of my mind, and bringing me into captivity to the law of sin which is in my members." The witnesses note the two laws at war. On "another law in my members," the grammatical voices note the opposing principle. Barnes and Robertson note a second "law" (principle) in the members (the body, the flesh) — the law of sin, opposed to the law of the mind.

On "warring against the law of my mind," the witnesses note the conflict. This law of sin wars (campaigns, fights) against the law of the mind (the renewed reason or higher self that delights in God's law, v.22) — open warfare within.

On "bringing me into captivity," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the bondage: the law of sin brings the self into captivity (takes it prisoner) — whether the believer's painful experience of remaining sin's power (regenerate view) or the convicted sinner's bondage (other view). The verse displays the two laws at war, the self brought into captivity to sin.

The witnesses agree: there is another law (principle) in the members (the flesh) — the law of sin — warring against the law of the mind (the renewed reason that delights in God's law, v.22), bringing the self into captivity to the law of sin (Barnes, Robertson). The schools read the captivity by their view: Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer's painful experience of remaining sin's power; Wesley and the Arminian read the convicted sinner's bondage. The verse displays the two laws at war. The verse marks the opposing law: sin in the members. The verse displays the warfare: against the mind. The verse displays the captivity: brought prisoner to sin. The verse displays the reading: by the view of the "I." The two laws at war display the open conflict within the man. The verse displays the law of sin: in the members. The verse displays the battle: against the mind. The verse displays the bondage: captivity to sin. The two laws at war — the law of sin in the members warring against the law of the mind and bringing the self into captivity — display the open conflict within: the renewed mind that delights in God's law is opposed by the law of sin in the flesh, which takes the self prisoner, whether the believer's grievous experience of remaining sin (Reformed) or the convicted sinner's bondage (Arminian). The verse displays the climax of the inward war, leading to the cry for deliverance (v.24).
24:
"O wretched man that I am! who shall deliver me from the body of this death?" The witnesses note the cry of the wretched man. On "O wretched man that I am," the grammatical voices note the anguish. Barnes and Robertson note the heart-rending cry (Sanday and Headlam: "from the depths of despair") — the climax of the conflict, the self overwhelmed by the struggle, longing for deliverance.

On "the body of this death," the witnesses note the burden. Barnes and Clarke note the striking image: possibly an allusion to the ancient tyrant's punishment of chaining a living man to a corpse — the body of sin and death, a loathsome burden the self drags about, longing to be free.

The schools read the cry by their view. The Reformed Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer bewailing his unhappy condition, panting for full deliverance while submitting to God; the wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the convicted sinner (or pre-conversion Paul) at the point of despair, just before deliverance. The verse displays the cry of the wretched man for deliverance from the body of death.

The witnesses agree the verse is the heart-rending cry of the wretched man — "from the depths of despair" (Barnes, Robertson) — the climax of the conflict, longing for deliverance from "the body of this death" (the body of sin and death, possibly alluding to the tyrant's punishment of chaining a living man to a corpse, the loathsome burden dragged about, Barnes, Clarke). The schools read it by their view: Calvin (regenerate) sees the believer bewailing his condition and panting for full deliverance while submitting to God; Clarke reads the convicted sinner or pre-conversion Paul at despair's point, just before deliverance. The verse displays the cry of the wretched man. The verse marks the anguish: O wretched man. The verse displays the longing: who shall deliver me? The verse displays the burden: the body of this death. The verse displays the reading: by the view of the "I." The cry displays the climax of the inward war and the longing for deliverance. The verse displays the despair: wretched man. The verse displays the burden: body of death. The verse displays the plea: who shall deliver? The cry of the wretched man — "who shall deliver me from the body of this death?" — displays the climax of the inward conflict: overwhelmed by the war with sin, the self cries out in anguish for deliverance from the loathsome burden of the body of sin and death, like a living man chained to a corpse. The verse displays the heart-rending plea that prepares for the answer of grace, and is answered with thanksgiving through Christ (v.25).
25:
"I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord. So then with the mind I serve the law of God; but with the flesh the law of sin." The witnesses note the deliverance through Christ and the summary of the conflict. On "I thank God through Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the answer. Barnes and Robertson note the cry (v.24) is answered: deliverance comes through Jesus Christ — thanksgiving to God for the rescue the law could not give. (Some manuscripts read "the grace of God," Clarke, Gill.)

On "with the mind I serve the law of God, but with the flesh the law of sin," the witnesses note the summary. Paul sums up the whole conflict: the same self, with the mind (the renewed inner man) serving God's law, but with the flesh serving the law of sin — the divided service.

The schools read it by their view, and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke argues strongly that the deliverance (ch. 8) shows the normal Christian state is victory, so ch. 7 describes the pre-deliverance state; the Reformed Calvin and Gill hold the believer continues this conflict through life, the deliverance being progressive and consummated at last. The verse displays the deliverance through Christ and the summary of the divided service.

The witnesses agree the cry is answered: "I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord" — deliverance comes through Christ, the rescue the law could not give (Barnes, Robertson; some manuscripts read "the grace of God," Clarke, Gill) — and Paul sums up the conflict: the same self serving God's law with the mind but the law of sin with the flesh. The schools read it by their view: Clarke argues the deliverance (ch. 8) shows the normal Christian state is victory, so ch. 7 describes the pre-deliverance state; Calvin and Gill hold the believer continues this conflict through life, the deliverance progressive and consummated at last. The verse displays the deliverance through Christ. The verse marks the answer: thanks through Christ. The verse displays the summary: mind serves God, flesh serves sin. The verse displays the debate: pre-deliverance or lifelong conflict. The verse displays the rescue: what the law could not do. The deliverance displays the answer to the cry and the summary of the divided service. The verse displays the thanksgiving: through Christ. The verse displays the division: mind and flesh. The verse displays the controversy: the Christian state. The deliverance through Christ and the summary of the divided service — "I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord... with the mind I serve the law of God, but with the flesh the law of sin" — display the answer to the wretched man's cry and the close of the great conflict: rescue comes not from the law but through Christ, even as the self acknowledges its divided service. The verse displays the turning-point from the bondage of Romans 7 to the liberty of the Spirit in Romans 8, with the schools divided on whether the conflict is the believer's lifelong experience or the state preceding full deliverance.
